text.title: contextual-inquiry Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for contextual-inquiry. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Conduct the session in the user's actual work environment, not a lab or meeting room.
rule.rationale: Context principle: removing the user from their environment removes the environmental triggers, physical artifacts, and interruptions that shape real behavior.

rule.statement: Treat the user as the expert and the researcher as the apprentice (learner). Use the opening script to establish this frame before observation begins.
rule.rationale: Partnership principle: the master-apprentice model reduces observer-effect distortion. Users perform naturally when they are the acknowledged authority.

rule.statement: Share interpretations aloud during the session and invite corrections: "So it seems like... Did I understand correctly?"
rule.rationale: Interpretation principle: researchers form wrong mental models continuously. Surfacing and correcting them in-session prevents compounded misinterpretations.

rule.statement: Enter each session with clear research questions. The observation guide must specify which aspects of work to observe and what questions to answer.
rule.rationale: Focus principle: without a defined lens, sessions produce unfocused notes. Clear focus enables selective attention without missing key phenomena.

rule.statement: Record these seven data types for every session: actions taken, tools used, artifacts created or used, physical environment description, interruptions, workarounds, and frustrations expressed verbally.
rule.rationale: Comprehensive capture prevents the most common loss: workarounds and physical artifacts that hold the real workflow are easy to overlook.

rule.statement: Mark inferences as inferences in notes from the very first session. Write observed vs inferred in separate fields; retroactive separation after analysis is impossible.
rule.rationale: Mixing observed and inferred data produces false confidence in downstream analysis.

rule.statement: Define focus areas, recruit 4-8 participants for one focus area, obtain organizational permission, prepare recording equipment, and create an observation guide before the first session.
rule.rationale: 4-8 sessions is the sweet spot for one focus area — enough to find patterns across participants without spending more sessions than new insights warrant.

rule.statement: Run a 1-hour pilot before the real round to fix observation-guide weaknesses. The second guide is always better.
rule.rationale: Guides written before observation contain leading questions and missing focus areas that only become visible when used in the field.

rule.statement: Watch silently first, then ask about specific observed actions: "Why did you do that?" Never suggest improvements or express surprise at workarounds during the session.
rule.rationale: Premature suggestions and expressed surprise alter behavior immediately.

rule.statement: Capture artifacts physically (photos with consent). Workflows rarely live entirely on screens; paper notes, whiteboard scribbles, and handwritten forms are primary data.
rule.rationale: Remote CI variants cannot capture physical artifacts, which is a significant data loss.

rule.statement: Debrief within 30 minutes of each session, while peripheral memory is intact. Audio recording does not replace immediate debrief.
rule.rationale: Peripheral memory (environmental details, emotional tone, gestural context) decays within hours.

rule.statement: After all sessions: identify patterns using affinity diagrams, sequence models, and cultural models.
rule.rationale: Single-session insights are anecdotes. Cross-session analysis transforms observations into findings.

rule.statement: When in-person access is not possible, use screen share plus webcam to capture some environmental context; supplement with self-documentation or diary study. Always acknowledge that remote CI loses physical artifacts and reduces environmental signal.
rule.rationale: Remote CI captures ~60-70% of the signal of in-person CI. The loss is concentrated in physical artifacts, peripheral activities, and environmental triggers.

rule.statement: Time-boxed sessions (1-2 h) miss low-frequency tasks (monthly close, quarterly audits) and emergencies. Sample for role, shift, seniority, and edge-case variation — not statistical validity.
rule.rationale: Contextual inquiry is not a representative sample; it is a qualitative deep dive. Selecting for variation ensures the findings cover the range of work patterns, not just the most common ones.

rule.statement: Observer effect is real; participants perform their work for the researcher, missing the messy reality. Master-apprentice framing assumes a single expert; team-based work needs a different choreography.
rule.rationale: Acknowledging observer effect and adapting method for team settings are part of rigorous CI practice. Design-by-observation without acknowledging these limits produces false confidence.

rule.statement: Run a 1-hour pilot before the real round to fix observation-guide weaknesses; the second guide is always better.
rule.rationale: Guides written before observation contain leading questions and missing focus areas that only become visible when used in the field.

rule.statement: Capture artifacts physically (photos with consent) — workflows rarely live entirely on screens.
rule.rationale: Paper notes, whiteboard scribbles, and handwritten forms are primary data that screen-only capture misses.

rule.statement: Mark inferences as inferences in notes from the very first session; retroactive separation of observed vs inferred is impossible.
rule.rationale: Mixing observed and inferred data produces false confidence. Downstream agents and designers treat both as equally grounded unless the distinction is explicit.

rule.statement: Debrief within 30 minutes of the session, while peripheral memory is intact. Audio recording does not replace this.
rule.rationale: Peripheral memory (environmental details, emotional tone, gestural context) decays within hours. Recordings capture only what the microphone picked up.

rule.statement: Sample for variation, not statistical validity: include shifts, regions, seniority, edge-case roles. 4-8 sessions is the sweet spot for one focus area.
rule.rationale: Contextual inquiry finds patterns across variation. Selecting only typical users produces findings that miss the edge cases that break system assumptions.

rule.statement: Share interim findings with participants ("member checking") to catch misinterpretations before downstream design work commits to them.
rule.rationale: Researchers form incorrect interpretations even with shared-interpretation protocol. Participant validation is the final check before findings become design inputs.

rule.statement: Treat workarounds as system-design signals, not user errors. The fact that someone keeps paper notes is the finding.
rule.rationale: Workarounds reveal where the designed system diverges from how work actually happens. Treating them as errors misses the most valuable design signals.
